TECHNICAL ANALYSIS

Intermediate Knowledge

Fibonacci Retracements

💡 I. Why should you use it ?

The Fibonacci retracement tool is one of the most used and useful method we can adopt in technical analysis. However, it can be highly complicated to apply since there are a lot of confusing guides on the internet. In this lesson, I'll try to make it easier to digest with the goal of helping you apply Fibonacci to your strategies. There's a lot that has to be said, especially regarding the story behind this tool, but I think it would be annoying and that’s why I’ll go straight to the concrete applications.

Fibonacci retracements tool is one of my favorite trading tools because, once understood, it’s simple to apply and provides a lot of amazing informations.

This sequence of numbers in fact, is like a self-fulfiling prophecy. Besides the mathematical connotation, a lot of traders look at the targets provided by this instrument, making it highly reliable for our operation.

In which areas could it help us?

  • Identifying support and resistance levels
  • Highlighting the strength of the move
  • Setting take profit targets
  • Removing emotions thus having an unbiased approach

To correctly apply the Fibonacci retracement tool, the first and most important step is knowing where to draw it from. The two key terms to understand here are swing high and swing low, which are essential in identifying the range of a price move (refer to the Market Structure section for more detail).

Once the swing high and swing low are defined, the next step is to determine the trend.

  • In an uptrend, you would draw the Fibonacci retracement from the swing low to the swing high, moving left to right.
  • In a downtrend, you do the opposite: start from the swing high and draw down to the swing low, again from left to right.

This setup helps identify potential areas where the price might pull back or reverse during its trend. But before exploring a few examples, let’s start from the basis, the settings.

I. Settings

To access the Fibonacci retracement tool on TradingView, start by navigating to the toolbar on the left-hand side of the screen. Look for the “Gann and Fibonacci tools” section.. it’s usually represented by a diagonal line icon.

Click on it to expand the list of available tools, and then select Fibonacci Retracement from the dropdown. TradingView offers several other tools in this section, but for the purposes of this method, I’ll focus exclusively on the Fibonacci retracement, as it’s the one I rely on personally. The others may be useful in specific strategies, but they’re not part of my workflow.

Now that you’ve selected the tool, it’s time to set things up properly. If you’re new to TradingView or technical analysis in general, this part can feel overwhelming, especially if you’re starting from a blank chart but don’t worry, in the next section, I’ll walk you through my personal setup step by step, including how I organize the chart, choose colors and levels, and configure everything for a clean and effective workflow.

When you first draw the Fibonacci retracement on your chart, you'll likely notice that the default appearance is quite colorful and visually cluttered. This can be distracting, especially when you're trying to focus on key price levels and keep your chart clean and readable. To improve clarity, it's important to customize the tool’s appearance.

After drawing the Fibonacci retracement, double-click on any of the retracement level numbers on the chart. This will open the settings panel for the tool. In the “Style” tab, you'll see a list of all the Fibonacci levels along with their corresponding values and color settings.

To simplify the visual layout and highlight the most critical levels, start by changing the color of all levels to black, except for three key ones: 0, 0.5, and 1.

These levels represent the start, midpoint, and end of the retracement zone and play a fundamental role in price analysis, so it's helpful to make them stand out. Change the color of these three levels to red to visually separate them from the others and draw attention to their importance.

There are also extended levels beyond 1.0, such as 1.272, 1.454, and 1.618. These are known as Fibonacci extension levels and are often used to project potential price targets beyond the original swing. While we’ll discuss how to color-code and use these levels in more detail later, it’s worth noting that they should also be given distinct colors so they’re easy to identify when analyzing potential breakout or continuation scenarios.

By cleaning up the visual style of the Fibonacci tool, you reduce distractions and ensure that only the most relevant levels stand out, which ultimately makes your analysis more efficient and your charts more readable.

Next, we need to do a bit more cleanup to further streamline the look of the Fibonacci retracement on your chart. Staying in the Style tab of the settings panel, you’ll notice a checkbox labeled “Background.”  By default, this is enabled, which adds a shaded area between the Fibonacci levels.

While this might seem helpful at first, it often clutters the chart and makes it harder to focus on price action. To keep your chart clean and distraction-free, uncheck the “Background” option. This will remove the colored shading between the levels, leaving only the lines and labels, which is all you really need for precise analysis.

Next, adjust the label position so that the retracement levels are easier to read. By default, labels may appear on the left or directly beside the levels, but for better readability and consistency, change the Label Placement to “Right” or “Middle Right.” This setting ensures that all Fibonacci level labels are aligned neatly along the right side of the tool, making them easier to follow, especially when the chart is zoomed out.

These small adjustments may seem minor, but they play a big role in keeping your Fibonacci tool visually clean and optimized for practical analysis. Once done, your chart will look much more professional, and you’ll be better equipped to focus on what really matters.

The final step in customizing your Fibonacci retracement tool is deciding whether or not to include the trendline.

TradingView gives you the option to display a connecting line between your two anchor points (the swing high and swing low( which visually emphasizes the direction of the move. Personally, I choose not to use the trendline.

While it can be helpful in some contexts, I find that it adds unnecessary clutter to the chart and takes away from the clean, focused look I aim for in my analysis. Ultimately, whether or not you include the trendline is a matter of personal preference. The key is to keep your chart readable and consistent with your overall trading approach.

The final result should be like this :

II. The numbers

Now let’s take a closer look at the significance of the Fibonacci levels themselves. It’s important to understand that each number in the Fibonacci retracement tool corresponds to a potential retracement zone, a price area where the market may either pause, reverse, or bounce.

These levels essentially act as dynamic support and resistance zones, helping us to anticipate possible reactions during a trending move.

Among these levels, 0, 0.5, and 1 are especially important. The 0 and 1 levels represent the starting and ending points of the measured move, typically a swing low and a swing high (or vice versa, depending on the trend direction). The 0.5 level, while not a true Fibonacci number, is widely used by traders as it marks the midpoint of the move.

It often serves as a psychological support or resistance area and can act as a critical decision zone. If the price breaks below the 0.5 level in an uptrend (or above it in a downtrend), it may indicate a deeper retracement or even a potential trend reversal. For that reason, many traders view the 0.5 line as a kind of watershed (me too)a dividing line between a healthy pullback and a more significant breakdown. In my own chart setup, I color these three levels (0, 0.5, and 1) in red to make them stand out clearly.

This visual emphasis helps me quickly identify the key structural points of the retracement.

As for the other Fibonacci levels, all of them are useful, but a few stand out as especially significant. One of the most notable is 0.618, often referred to as the “golden pocket” (GP). Alongside it, the levels that I value the most are the 0.786, the 0.705 (algorithmic fib) and the 0.886. Occasionally, you might come across Fibonacci levels that include a negative sign, such as -0.618. These are part of what's known as the Fibonacci extension levels, which project potential price targets beyond the starting or ending point of the retracement. These negative values are derived using the same mathematical principles, simply extended into negative space.

While they can be useful in certain strategies, especially for projecting future price moves, make sure you’re aware of the difference so you don’t confuse them with the standard retracement levels.

III. Common uses

While there are numerous areas commonly used by traders and analysts, in this post, I've shared my personal preferences.

  • Weekly Macro Chart (example with BTC)

Simply attach it from the macro swing high to the macro swing low dragging it to the right. If we analyze the chart more deeply, we can clearly see how the Fibonacci retracement areas have been useful to identify crucial levels.

Just look at how the price has reacted to the 0.5 and the 0.382 range, finding both a strong resistance and a strong support. Works like magic, doesn’t it? This is a simple method to find key areas for every asset you decide to analyze, in a “retail manner”.

“Retail manner? Why?” Because this is the most common use of the Fibonacci tool, simply dragging it on macro so these levels are widely watched. This doesn’t mean they’re invalid, but there’s much more to know, something you’ll learn as long as you will study this Notion.

  • Shorter Timeframe

The Fibonacci tool could be used on each timeframe you prefer, there’s no “limit” to its use as it truly depends on your trading style and what you want to calculate/analyze.

In this 4H example I took the range formed between 110.000$ and 103.000$ where you can clearly see the reaction toward the 0.786 with multiple tests of the EQ, a “watershed” for the development of the price action since continuing to holding above would have confirmed the desire from smart money to push the price higher.

As a general rule, like explained in the discount/premium section, your goal should be to sell/look for shorts above the EQ and buy/long when the price is being traded at discount levels. Naturally, this is a LTF range so, as always, the priority goes to HTF.

  • Trending up market

We just saw a few examples with the market trending down, but what if we want to use it in an uptrend? Let’s take the SOL 1W chart

Contrary to the downtrend where the Fibonacci was dragged from the swing high to the swing low, in this uptrend I inverted it and drew it from the swing low (1) to the swing high (0). Look at how the price has used the most important levels to retrace during this uptrend.

From the peak at 32$ it has found its macro support at the 0.786, bounced from there and pushed to the 0.382, back to the 0.618 (golden pocket, remember?) and then a big breakout.

  • Mixing up everything and finding areas of interest in uptrends

Ever wondered how to identify the next potential targets during a bullish trend ?

The first step is to identify your area of interest within the trend, which gives you a strategic zone to watch for potential entries or continuation moves. In an uptrend, I start by drawing the Fibonacci retracement from the swing low to the swing high of the current move. This defines the entire range of the price action you're analyzing.

Once the retracement is in place, I focus specifically on the zone between the 0.382 and 0.618 levels. This range is often referred to as the “golden zone” and is where many professional traders expect the price to pull back before continuing higher.

It represents a healthy retracement within an overall bullish structure, not too shallow, not too deep. This zone becomes my area of interest because it's where buyers are most likely to step in and defend the trend. In many cases, you’ll notice this area aligns closely with previous highs, consolidation zones, or support, providing an additional confluence and confidence in the setup.

Logically, this is my area of interest which is never taken alone, but I always look for further alignment with price action, but this is something that belongs to other sections of the Notion.

  • Finding potential upside targets

To project future price targets in a bullish trend, I use the same Fibonacci tool, but in reverse. This method is commonly referred to as the “Fibonacci reverse sequence.” It allows us to identify potential extension levels where price may rally after a pullback or consolidation.

Using the same chart, instead of drawing the Fibonacci retracement from the swing low to the swing high, I reverse the direction and draw it from the swing high to the swing low. This flips the tool, revealing key extension levels above the previous high, levels that act as potential upside targets.

Once the reverse Fibonacci is in place, my primary area of interest becomes the zone between the 1.272, 1.454 and 1.618 levels.

This range is statistically significant, as it often marks the price zone where bullish momentum extends after a confirmed breakout. These levels are not only widely used by traders, but I backtested them during time and I found them very reliable.

It’s a practical and proven method to forecast possible price objectives with confidence.

IV. OTZ

Another Fibonacci setting that I use quite often and that comes from the “ICT” school is the OTZ. OTZ means “Optimal Trading Zone” and is another Fibonacci sequence to calculate retracements and targets.

The first step is setting the Fibonacci with the levels plotted on the right (1, -1, -0.62, -0.27, 0, 0.5, 0.62, 0.705, 0.79) ~> look for “Template” and add it with the name “OTZ” so you'll always have this setting saved.

The second step is drawing it from the swing low to the swing high to calculate a retracement in an uptrend where the OTZ goes from the 0.62 to the 0.79. That is the zone. To make it relevant however, on the left there might be at least a FVG that adds confluence, otherwise the OTZ isn't really valid.

The above levels (-0.27, -0.62. -1) are the TP targets.

This of course can be used even to calculate entries in a downtrend, therefore drawing it from the swing high to the swing low.

V. Negative Fibonacci

While browsing through other traders’ charts, you may occasionally encounter what’s called the “negative Fibonacci sequence.” 

This approach is quite similar to the reverse Fibonacci extension method I explained earlier, which involves levels like 1.272, 1.454, and 1.618, but with a twist. The negative Fibonacci sequence applies the classic Fibonacci retracement levels, only this time projecting them into the negative territory. In practical terms, this means adding a “-” (minus) sign in front of the usual Fibonacci levels to create a negative projection.

This technique allows you to explore potential price targets or support/resistance zones that lie below the current swing low. To streamline your workflow, I strongly recommend saving this setup as a template. Simply click on the “Template”option in your Fibonacci tool settings and save it under the name “Negative Fibonacci.” 

This way, you’ll always have a clean, organized, and ready-to-use version of the negative Fibonacci sequence at your fingertips.

So, when to use it?

Essentially, as said before, it is similar to the inverse sequence and helps calculate targets in an uptrend when you don’t have previous PA to help you (like previous support/resistance).

To do so, simply draw the Fibonacci from the SL to the SH, so you will obtain targets all the way up in an “unexplored” condition.

Remember, as per the classic Fibonacci, that the equilibrium point is one of the most important if not the most important levels to rely on, so take it into consideration during your analyses.
